sourceRefs.fallbackText: Fielding, et al.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless application-level protocol for distributed, collaborative, hypertext information systems. This document defines HTTP caches and the associated header fields that control cache behavior or indicate cacheable response messages. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless application-level request/response protocol that uses extensible semantics and self-descriptive messages for flexible interaction with network-based hypertext information systems. It is typically used for distributed information systems, where the use of response caches can improve performance. This docum
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless application-level request/response protocol that uses extensible semantics and self-descriptive messages for flexible interaction with network-based hypertext informa

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An HTTP "cache" is a local store of response messages and the subsystem that controls storage, retrieval, and deletion of messages in it. A cache stores cacheable responses to reduce the response time and network bandwidth consumption on future equivalent requests. Any client or server MAY use a cache, though not when acting as a tunnel ( Section 3.7 of [ HT
sourceRefs.textQuote: An HTTP "cache" is a local store of response messages and the subsystem that controls storage, retrieval, and deletion of messages in it. A cache stores cacheable responses to reduce the response time and network bandwid

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A "shared cache" is a cache that stores responses for reuse by more than one user; shared caches are usually (but not always) deployed as a part of an intermediary. A "private cache", in contrast, is dedicated to a single user; often, they are deployed as a component of a user agent. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: A "shared cache" is a cache that stores responses for reuse by more than one user; shared caches are usually (but not always) deployed as a part of an intermediary. A "private cache", in contrast, is dedicated to a singl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The goal of HTTP caching is significantly improving performance by reusing a prior response message to satisfy a current request. A cache considers a stored response "fresh", as defined in Section 4.2 , if it can be reused without "validation" (checking with the origin server to see if the cached response remains valid for this request). A fresh response can
sourceRefs.textQuote: The goal of HTTP caching is significantly improving performance by reusing a prior response message to satisfy a current request. A cache considers a stored response "fresh", as defined in Section 4.2 , if it can be reus

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This document obsoletes RFC 7234 , with the changes being summarized in Appendix B . ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: This document obsoletes RFC 7234 , with the changes being summarized in Appendix B . ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The key words " MUST ", " MUST NOT ", " REQUIRED ", " SHALL ", " SHALL NOT ", " SHOULD ", " SHOULD NOT ", " RECOMMENDED ", " NOT RECOMMENDED ", " MAY ", and " OPTIONAL " in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 [ RFC2119 ] [ RFC8174 ] when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The key words " MUST ", " MUST NOT ", " REQUIRED ", " SHALL ", " SHALL NOT ", " SHOULD ", " SHOULD NOT ", " RECOMMENDED ", " NOT RECOMMENDED ", " MAY ", and " OPTIONAL " in this document are to be interpreted as describe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Section 2 of [ HTTP ] defines conformance criteria and contains considerations regarding error handling. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Section 2 of [ HTTP ] defines conformance criteria and contains considerations regarding error handling. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This specification uses the Augmented Backus-Naur Form (ABNF) notation of [ RFC5234 ] , extended with the notation for case-sensitivity in strings defined in [ RFC7405 ] . ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: This specification uses the Augmented Backus-Naur Form (ABNF) notation of [ RFC5234 ] , extended with the notation for case-sensitivity in strings defined in [ RFC7405 ] . ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: It also uses a list extension, defined in Section 5.6.1 of [ HTTP ] , that allows for compact definition of comma-separated lists using a "#" operator (similar to how the "*" operator indicates repetition). Appendix A shows the collected grammar with all list operators expanded to standard ABNF notation. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: It also uses a list extension, defined in Section 5.6.1 of [ HTTP ] , that allows for compact definition of comma-separated lists using a "#" operator (similar to how the "*" operator indicates repetition). Appendix A sh

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The following core rule is included by reference, as defined in [ RFC5234 ], Appendix B.1 : DIGIT (decimal 0-9). ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The following core rule is included by reference, as defined in [ RFC5234 ], Appendix B.1 : DIGIT (decimal 0-9). ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: [ HTTP ] defines the following rules: ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: [ HTTP ] defines the following rules: ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: HTTP-date = <HTTP-date, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.7> OWS = <OWS, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.3> field-name = <field-name, see [HTTP], Section 5.1> quoted-string = <quoted-string, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.4> token = <token, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.2>
sourceRefs.textQuote: HTTP-date = <HTTP-date, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.7> OWS = <OWS, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.3> field-name = <field-name, see [HTTP], Section 5.1> quoted-string = <quoted-string, see [HTTP], Section 5.6.4> token = <token, see

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The delta-seconds rule specifies a non-negative integer, representing time in seconds. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The delta-seconds rule specifies a non-negative integer, representing time in seconds. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: delta-seconds = 1*DIGIT
sourceRefs.textQuote: delta-seconds = 1*DIGIT

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A recipient parsing a delta-seconds value and converting it to binary form ought to use an arithmetic type of at least 31 bits of non-negative integer range. If a cache receives a delta-seconds value greater than the greatest integer it can represent, or if any of its subsequent calculations overflows, the cache MUST consider the value to be 2147483648 (2 31
sourceRefs.textQuote: A recipient parsing a delta-seconds value and converting it to binary form ought to use an arithmetic type of at least 31 bits of non-negative integer range. If a cache receives a delta-seconds value greater than the gre

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note: The value 2147483648 is here for historical reasons, represents infinity (over 68 years), and does not need to be stored in binary form; an implementation could produce it as a string if any overflow occurs, even if the calculations are performed with an arithmetic type incapable of directly representing that number. What matters here is that an overfl
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note: The value 2147483648 is here for historical reasons, represents infinity (over 68 years), and does not need to be stored in binary form; an implementation could produce it as a string if any overflow occurs, even i

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Proper cache operation preserves the semantics of HTTP transfers while reducing the transmission of information already held in the cache. See Section 3 of [ HTTP ] for the general terminology and core concepts of HTTP. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Proper cache operation preserves the semantics of HTTP transfers while reducing the transmission of information already held in the cache. See Section 3 of [ HTTP ] for the general terminology and core concepts of HTTP.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Although caching is an entirely OPTIONAL feature of HTTP, it can be assumed that reusing a cached response is desirable and that such reuse is the default behavior when no requirement or local configuration prevents it. Therefore, HTTP cache requirements are focused on preventing a cache from either storing a non-reusable response or reusing a stored respons
sourceRefs.textQuote: Although caching is an entirely OPTIONAL feature of HTTP, it can be assumed that reusing a cached response is desirable and that such reuse is the default behavior when no requirement or local configuration prevents it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The "cache key" is the information a cache uses to choose a response and is composed from, at a minimum, the request method and target URI used to retrieve the stored response; the method determines under which circumstances that response can be used to satisfy a subsequent request. However, many HTTP caches in common use today only cache GET responses and t
sourceRefs.textQuote: The "cache key" is the information a cache uses to choose a response and is composed from, at a minimum, the request method and target URI used to retrieve the stored response; the method determines under which circumsta

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A cache might store multiple responses for a request target that is subject to content negotiation. Caches differentiate these responses by incorporating some of the original request's header fields into the cache key as well, using information in the Vary response header field, as per Section 4.1 . ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: A cache might store multiple responses for a request target that is subject to content negotiation. Caches differentiate these responses by incorporating some of the original request's header fields into the cache key as

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Caches might incorporate additional material into the cache key. For example, user agent caches might include the referring site's identity, thereby "double keying" the cache to avoid some privacy risks (see Section 7.2 ). ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Caches might incorporate additional material into the cache key. For example, user agent caches might include the referring site's identity, thereby "double keying" the cache to avoid some privacy risks (see Section 7.2

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Most commonly, caches store the successful result of a retrieval request: i.e., a 200 (OK) response to a GET request, which contains a representation of the target resource ( Section 9.3.1 of [ HTTP ] ). However, it is also possible to store redirects, negative results (e.g., 404 (Not Found)), incomplete results (e.g., 206 (Partial Content)), and responses t
sourceRefs.textQuote: Most commonly, caches store the successful result of a retrieval request: i.e., a 200 (OK) response to a GET request, which contains a representation of the target resource ( Section 9.3.1 of [ HTTP ] ). However, it is a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A cache is "disconnected" when it cannot contact the origin server or otherwise find a forward path for a request. A disconnected cache can serve stale responses in some circumstances ( Section 4.2.4 ). ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: A cache is "disconnected" when it cannot contact the origin server or otherwise find a forward path for a request. A disconnected cache can serve stale responses in some circumstances ( Section 4.2.4 ). ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A cache MUST NOT store a response to a request unless: ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: A cache MUST NOT store a response to a request unless: ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the request method is understood by the cache; ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: the request method is understood by the cache; ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the response status code is final (see Section 15 of [ HTTP ] ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: the response status code is final (see Section 15 of [ HTTP ] ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if the response status code is 206 or 304, or the must-understand cache directive (see Section 5.2.2.3 ) is present: the cache understands the response status code; ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: if the response status code is 206 or 304, or the must-understand cache directive (see Section 5.2.2.3 ) is present: the cache understands the response status code; ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the no-store cache directive is not present in the response (see Section 5.2.2.5 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: the no-store cache directive is not present in the response (see Section 5.2.2.5 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if the cache is shared: the private response directive is either not present or allows a shared cache to store a modified response; see Section 5.2.2.7 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: if the cache is shared: the private response directive is either not present or allows a shared cache to store a modified response; see Section 5.2.2.7 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if the cache is shared: the Authorization header field is not present in the request (see Section 11.6.2 of [ HTTP ] ) or a response directive is present that explicitly allows shared caching (see Section 3.5 ); and ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: if the cache is shared: the Authorization header field is not present in the request (see Section 11.6.2 of [ HTTP ] ) or a response directive is present that explicitly allows shared caching (see Section 3.5 ); and ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: the response contains at least one of the following: ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: the response contains at least one of the following: ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: a public response directive (see Section 5.2.2.9 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: a public response directive (see Section 5.2.2.9 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: a private response directive, if the cache is not shared (see Section 5.2.2.7 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: a private response directive, if the cache is not shared (see Section 5.2.2.7 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: an Expires header field (see Section 5.3 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: an Expires header field (see Section 5.3 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: a max-age response directive (see Section 5.2.2.1 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: a max-age response directive (see Section 5.2.2.1 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if the cache is shared: an s-maxage response directive (see Section 5.2.2.10 ); ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: if the cache is shared: an s-maxage response directive (see Section 5.2.2.10 ); ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: a cache extension that allows it to be cached (see Section 5.2.3 ); or ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: a cache extension that allows it to be cached (see Section 5.2.3 ); or ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: a status code that is defined as heuristically cacheable (see Section 4.2.2 ). ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: a status code that is defined as heuristically cacheable (see Section 4.2.2 ). ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that a cache extension can override any of the requirements listed; see Section 5.2.3 . ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that a cache extension can override any of the requirements listed; see Section 5.2.3 . ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In this context, a cache has "understood" a request method or a response status code if it recognizes it and implements all specified caching-related behavior. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: In this context, a cache has "understood" a request method or a response status code if it recognizes it and implements all specified caching-related behavior. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that, in normal operation, some caches will not store a response that has neither a cache validator nor an explicit expiration time, as such responses are not usually useful to store. However, caches are not prohibited from storing such responses. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that, in normal operation, some caches will not store a response that has neither a cache validator nor an explicit expiration time, as such responses are not usually useful to store. However, caches are not prohibi

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Caches MUST include all received response header fields -- including unrecognized ones -- when storing a response; this assures that new HTTP header fields can be successfully deployed. However, the following exceptions are made: ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Caches MUST include all received response header fields -- including unrecognized ones -- when storing a response; this assures that new HTTP header fields can be successfully deployed. However, the following exceptions

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Connection header field and fields whose names are listed in it are required by Section 7.6.1 of [ HTTP ] to be removed before forwarding the message. This MAY be implemented by doing so before storage. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Connection header field and fields whose names are listed in it are required by Section 7.6.1 of [ HTTP ] to be removed before forwarding the message. This MAY be implemented by doing so before storage. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Likewise, some fields' semantics require them to be removed before forwarding the message, and this MAY be implemented by doing so before storage; see Section 7.6.1 of [ HTTP ] for some examples. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Likewise, some fields' semantics require them to be removed before forwarding the message, and this MAY be implemented by doing so before storage; see Section 7.6.1 of [ HTTP ] for some examples. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The no-cache ( Section 5.2.2.4 ) and private ( Section 5.2.2.7 ) cache directives can have arguments that prevent storage of header fields by all caches and shared caches, respectively. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The no-cache ( Section 5.2.2.4 ) and private ( Section 5.2.2.7 ) cache directives can have arguments that prevent storage of header fields by all caches and shared caches, respectively. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Header fields that are specific to the proxy that a cache uses when forwarding a request MUST NOT be stored, unless the cache incorporates the identity of the proxy into the cache key. Effectively, this is limited to Proxy-Authenticate ( Section 11.7.1 of [ HTTP ] ), Proxy-Authentication-Info ( Section 11.7.3 of [ HTTP ] ), and Proxy-Authorization ( Section
sourceRefs.textQuote: Header fields that are specific to the proxy that a cache uses when forwarding a request MUST NOT be stored, unless the cache incorporates the identity of the proxy into the cache key. Effectively, this is limited to Pro

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Caches MAY either store trailer fields separate from header fields or discard them. Caches MUST NOT combine trailer fields with header fields. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Caches MAY either store trailer fields separate from header fields or discard them. Caches MUST NOT combine trailer fields with header fields. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Caches are required to update a stored response's header fields from another (typically newer) response in several situations; for example, see Sections 3.4 , 4.3.4 , and 4.3.5 . ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Caches are required to update a stored response's header fields from another (typically newer) response in several situations; for example, see Sections 3.4 , 4.3.4 , and 4.3.5 . ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When doing so, the cache MUST add each header field in the provided response to the stored response, replacing field values that are already present, with the following exceptions: ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: When doing so, the cache MUST add each header field in the provided response to the stored response, replacing field values that are already present, with the following exceptions: ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Header fields excepted from storage in Section 3.1 , ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Header fields excepted from storage in Section 3.1 , ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Header fields that the cache's stored response depends upon, as described below, ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Header fields that the cache's stored response depends upon, as described below, ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Header fields that are automatically processed and removed by the recipient, as described below, and ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Header fields that are automatically processed and removed by the recipient, as described below, and ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Content-Length header field. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Content-Length header field. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In some cases, caches (especially in user agents) store the results of processing the received response, rather than the response itself, and updating header fields that affect that processing can result in inconsistent behavior and security issues. Caches in this situation MAY omit these header fields from updating stored responses on an exceptional basis b
sourceRefs.textQuote: In some cases, caches (especially in user agents) store the results of processing the received response, rather than the response itself, and updating header fields that affect that processing can result in inconsistent

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Furthermore, some fields are automatically processed and removed by the HTTP implementation, such as the Content-Range header field. Implementations MAY automatically omit such header fields from updates, even when the processing does not actually occur. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Note that the Content-* prefix is not a signal that a header field is omitted from update; it is a convention for MIME header fields, not HTTP. ¶
sourceRefs.textQuote: Note that the Content-* prefix is not a signal that a header field is omitted from update; it is a convention for MIME header fields, not HTTP. ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the request method is GET, the response status code is 200 (OK), and the entire response header section has been received, a cache MAY store a response that is not complete ( Section 6.1 of [ HTTP ] ) provided that the stored response is recorded as being incomplete. sourceRefs.fallbackText: A shared cache MUST NOT use a cached response to a request with an Authorization header field ( Section 11.6.2 of [ HTTP ] ) to satisfy any subsequent request unless the response contains a Cache-Control field with a response directive ( Section 5.2.2 ) that allows it to be stored by a shared cache, and the cache conforms to the requirements of that directiv

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In this specification, the following response directives have such an effect: must-revalidate ( Section 5.2.2.2 ), public ( Section 5.2.2.9 ), and s-maxage ( Section 5.2.2.10 ). ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When a stored response is used to satisfy a request without validation, a cache MUST generate an Age header field ( Section 5.1 ), replacing any present in the response with a value equal to the stored response's current_age; see Section 4.2.3 . ¶

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A cache MUST write through requests with methods that are unsafe ( Section 9.2.1 of [ HTTP ] ) to the origin server; i.e., a cache is not allowed to generate a reply to such a request before having forwarded the request and having received a corresponding response. ¶
